metadata.title: Explore a Mangrove Shore
metadata.summary: An interactive cross-section introduces how tides, muddy sediment and specialised roots shape a mangrove habitat.
metadata.subject: humanities
metadata.learningObjective: Describe how conditions change from the seaward edge to higher ground and relate visible mangrove adaptations to those conditions.

components.text: How do mangroves cope where land meets sea?
Explore the shore from left to right. After opening each marker, identify one challenge and one feature that helps organisms survive it.

components.prompt: Tap each region of the mangrove cross-section to reveal its conditions and adaptations.
components.altText: A side view from open seawater across a muddy intertidal mangrove shore to higher land, showing prop roots, breathing roots, mudskippers and leaf litter.

hotspots.label: Tidal channel
hotspots.reveal: Tides repeatedly cover and uncover the shore, changing water depth and salinity and carrying sediment and nutrients.

hotspots.label: Soft, oxygen-poor mud
hotspots.reveal: Fine sediment traps water but contains little oxygen. Animals burrow, while some trees send specialised roots above the surface for gas exchange.

hotspots.label: Prop roots
hotspots.reveal: Arching prop roots brace a tree in unstable mud and slow the water, which encourages more sediment to settle.

hotspots.label: Breathing roots
hotspots.reveal: Pencil-like roots project above the mud so gases can move between the root system and the air when the tide is low.

hotspots.label: Higher ground
hotspots.reveal: This part floods less often, so it is usually less salty and waterlogged than the seaward edge.
